heroOverview: Flushing is a transient, episodic reddening and warmth of the face, neck, and upper chest caused by sudden dilation of superficial blood vessels, a process called cutaneous vasodilation. Unlike a blush, which is brief and emotional in origin, pathological flushing recurs frequently, may occur without an obvious emotional trigger, and can be accompanied by sweating, palpitations, or diarrhoea that point toward an underlying systemic cause. Up to 30% of the general population experience occasional benign flushing, but persistent or severe episodes affect a clinically significant subset and require investigation to exclude treatable conditions such as rosacea, carcinoid tumour, phaeochromocytoma, mastocytosis, or menopause-related vasomotor instability. The specific characteristics of a flush — its timing, distribution, precipitating factors, and associated symptoms — are the key to identifying the underlying mechanism and guiding treatment.

keyStats.prevalence: Occasional flushing is experienced by an estimated 20–30% of adults; persistent pathological flushing is far less common and its prevalence depends on the underlying cause
keyStats.demographics: Vasomotor (menopausal) flushing affects approximately 75–85% of women around menopause; rosacea — the most common dermatological cause — affects about 5% of the global population
keyStats.avgAge: Menopausal flushing peaks around ages 50–55; rosacea peaks at 30–60; carcinoid syndrome is most common at 55–65
keyStats.globalCases: Rosacea affects an estimated 415 million people worldwide (ROSCO, 2019); carcinoid syndrome, a major systemic cause, affects roughly 1–2 per 100,000

definition: Flushing (ICD-10: R23.2) is a symptom — an episodic or persistent reddening of the skin due to abnormal dilation of cutaneous blood vessels — rather than a diagnosis in itself. The flush typically affects the face, ears, neck, and upper chest, reflecting the high density of cutaneous vessels in these areas. It is classified as 'wet' when sweating accompanies the redness (suggesting adrenergic nervous system involvement, as in phaeochromocytoma, anxiety, or carcinoid) or 'dry' when there is no sweating (suggesting direct vascular effects, as in niacin flushing, rosacea, and some carcinoid variants). Duration ranges from seconds (emotional blush) to hours (carcinoid flush, prolonged mastocytosis episode). The pathophysiology depends on the underlying cause but ultimately involves release of vasodilatory mediators — prostaglandins, serotonin, histamine, substance P, vasoactive intestinal peptide, or bradykinin — that act on dermal arterioles and capillaries. Physiological flushing — from heat, exercise, alcohol, spicy food, emotional excitement, and sexual arousal — is entirely normal. Flushing crosses several specialty domains: it is managed by general medicine, dermatology, endocrinology, and gastroenterology depending on the underlying cause.

typesClassification.description: Triggered by identifiable external factors — exercise, heat, alcohol, spicy food, embarrassment — and resolves within minutes without systemic symptoms. No investigation is required.

Recurrent facial flushing on a background of persistent central facial redness, telangiectasia, and sometimes papules or rhinophyma; triggered by alcohol, temperature, sunlight, and emotional stimuli. Affects approximately 5% of adults.

The most prevalent pathological flushing; abrupt episodes of intense heat, sweating, and skin redness caused by oestrogen withdrawal affecting hypothalamic thermoregulation; typically last 1–5 minutes and affect 75–85% of peri- and postmenopausal women.

Episodic facial redness, sometimes with a dusky cyanotic hue progressing to telangiectasia over years; caused by serotonin, bradykinin, and other mediators from a serotonin-secreting neuroendocrine (carcinoid) tumour. Often associated with diarrhoea and wheeze.

Episodic 'wet' flushing with profuse sweating, severe headache, hypertension, and palpitations caused by catecholamine surges from an adrenal medullary tumour; episodes last minutes to an hour and can be triggered by posture change, physical exertion, or straining.
Urticaria pigmentosa skin lesions that flush dramatically (Darier's sign) on rubbing, with systemic episodes of flushing, itching, diarrhoea, and hypotension from mast cell histamine release.

A wide range of medications — niacin (nicotinic acid), calcium-channel blockers, phosphodiesterase inhibitors, vancomycin (red man syndrome), opiates, and alcohol with metronidazole — cause flushing through direct vasodilation or prostaglandin release.

primarySymptoms: Sudden, episodic reddening of the face, ears, neck, and upper chest, often described as a wave of heat that rises from the chest upward within seconds.
A sensation of intense warmth or burning on the skin surface, disproportionate to ambient temperature, lasting from under a minute to several hours depending on the cause.
Sweating concurrent with or immediately after the red flush, particularly in menopausal hot flushes, phaeochromocytoma, and anxiety-related flushing.
Visible skin redness or blotchiness that fades within minutes in benign forms, or persists as background redness with overlying episodic deepening in rosacea.
Palpitations — awareness of a rapid or forceful heartbeat — accompanying vasomotor flushes, reflecting the compensatory sympathetic response to sudden peripheral vasodilation.
Diarrhoea occurring in association with flushing episodes, a combination strongly suggestive of carcinoid syndrome or mastocytosis where gut mediator release accompanies skin vasodilation.
Headache, particularly severe and throbbing, concurrent with flushing and sweating — a combination that raises the possibility of phaeochromocytoma and warrants urgent blood pressure measurement.
Itching or urticaria alongside skin redness, pointing toward histamine-mediated causes such as mastocytosis or allergic flushing.
Dizziness or near-fainting (pre-syncope) during a flush episode, reflecting a transient drop in blood pressure from widespread peripheral vasodilation.
Bronchospasm — wheeze or shortness of breath — during an episode of flushing; when present with diarrhoea and facial redness this triad is characteristic of carcinoid syndrome.
earlyWarningSigns: Flushing episodes occurring more than 5 times per day without an obvious external trigger such as heat, exercise, or alcohol.
A flush that leaves behind a background of persistent facial redness rather than the skin returning completely to baseline.
New onset of episodes accompanied by any systemic symptom — diarrhoea, headache, wheeze, or palpitations — that suggests a mediator-secreting tumour.
A documented rise in blood pressure during a flush episode, which raises the possibility of phaeochromocytoma.
emergencySigns: Severe headache, chest pain, and blood pressure above 180/120 mmHg during or immediately after a flush episode — possible hypertensive crisis from phaeochromocytoma.
Flushing accompanied by throat or tongue swelling, difficulty breathing, wheeze, and rapidly falling blood pressure — anaphylaxis, requiring immediate epinephrine and emergency care.
Profound dizziness, loss of consciousness, or collapse during a flush episode, suggesting severe hypotension or a carcinoid or mastocytosis crisis.
Flushing with chest pain and haemodynamic instability, particularly in a known carcinoid patient undergoing a procedure or physical stress — carcinoid crisis.

causes.description: Declining oestrogen destabilises the hypothalamic thermoregulatory centre, narrowing the neutral thermoregulatory zone so that small core temperature rises trigger an exaggerated vasodilatory heat-dissipating response perceived as a hot flush. This is the most common cause of pathological flushing in women aged 45–60, affecting 75–85% around the menopause transition.

Rosacea involves abnormal neurovascular reactivity with heightened skin sensitivity, and vascular rosacea (subtype 1) produces intense episodic flushing on a background of persistent central facial erythema. Triggers include UV light, alcohol, temperature extremes, spicy food, and skin care products; flushing causes enduring telangiectasia over years from repeated vascular injury.

Well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumours — particularly of the ileum, appendix, or bronchus — secrete serotonin, bradykinin, tachykinins, and prostaglandins into the systemic circulation once hepatic metastases are present. The resulting carcinoid syndrome produces episodic flushing (often with a distinctive salmon-pink colour), watery diarrhoea, bronchospasm, and tricuspid valve disease.

An adrenal (or less commonly extra-adrenal) catecholamine-secreting tumour releases surges of adrenaline and noradrenaline that cause episodic hypertension, palpitations, sweating, and flushing. Episodes last minutes to an hour and are provoked by posture change, palpation, micturition, or straining. Phaeochromocytoma causes approximately 0.1–0.2% of hypertension.

causes.cause: Mastocytosis and mast cell activation syndrome
causes.description: Abnormal proliferation or excessive activation of mast cells releases histamine, prostaglandin D2, and tryptase, causing episodic flushing, urticaria, abdominal cramping, diarrhoea, and — in systemic mastocytosis — hypotension. Darier's sign (urticarial whealing of skin lesions on friction) is characteristic.

Niacin (nicotinic acid) causes prostaglandin-mediated cutaneous flushing in approximately 70–90% of users, peaking 15–30 minutes after ingestion; aspirin taken 30 minutes before blunts this effect. Calcium-channel blockers, phosphodiesterase inhibitors (sildenafil), opioids, vancomycin ('red man syndrome'), and the combination of alcohol with disulfiram or metronidazole are other common pharmacological causes.

Alcohol inhibits aldehyde dehydrogenase — more profoundly in people of East Asian descent due to a common ALDH2 variant affecting up to 35% of this population — causing acetaldehyde accumulation that triggers intense facial flushing, nausea, and tachycardia. Spicy food (capsaicin), sodium nitrite in cured meats, and monosodium glutamate are other dietary triggers of cutaneous vasodilation.

riskFactors.description: Oestrogen decline during the menopause transition is the single most prevalent cause of pathological flushing; up to 85% of menopausal women experience hot flushes, and up to 25% find them severely disruptive to daily life and sleep.

Light-skinned individuals — particularly those of Northern European descent — have a higher density of visible superficial vessels and are more prone to rosacea-associated flushing; facial erythema is more visible in fair skin but skin vessel reactivity is also intrinsically higher in rosacea-prone individuals.

Alcohol is a potent cutaneous vasodilator and a near-universal trigger for flushing in rosacea; ALDH2-deficient individuals have an especially marked flush response. Reducing intake significantly reduces flush frequency in both rosacea and Asian flush.

A common ALDH2*2 variant in people of East Asian descent impairs acetaldehyde metabolism, causing the alcohol flush reaction in 35–40% of this population. Chronic exposure may raise oesophageal cancer risk by allowing acetaldehyde accumulation.

High baseline blood pressure amplifies the cardiovascular response to vasodilatory stimuli; the association with phaeochromocytoma-related flushing is direct, and blood pressure measurement during an episode is an important diagnostic step.

riskFactors.factor: Known neuroendocrine tumour or history thereof
riskFactors.description: Patients with a personal or family history of multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1 (MEN1) or carcinoid tumour are at risk of carcinoid syndrome, and unexplained flushing should be investigated even before systemic symptoms appear.

Multiple common drugs induce flushing through direct vasodilation or prostaglandin release; reviewing the medication list with a clinician and substituting or adjusting doses can abolish drug-induced flushing.

Sympathetic activation during panic episodes causes cutaneous vasodilation and sweating that mimic pathological flushing; they can be distinguished by the accompanying psychological symptoms and normal biochemical markers.

affectedDemographics: Peri- and postmenopausal women aged 45–60, the largest single group with pathological flushing
Adults with rosacea, predominantly fair-skinned individuals aged 30–60
People of East Asian descent with ALDH2 polymorphism who flush on any alcohol exposure
Patients with carcinoid tumours, typically aged 55–65 at diagnosis
People with phaeochromocytoma, most commonly diagnosed at ages 30–50
Individuals taking niacin, calcium-channel blockers, or other vasodilatory medications
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosing the cause of flushing depends heavily on characterising the episodes in detail: their duration, frequency, precipitating triggers, distribution on the body, whether sweating accompanies them ('wet' versus 'dry'), and what associated symptoms occur (diarrhoea, headache, wheeze, palpitations, hypertension). The clinical history alone can suggest the most likely category — for example, a menopausal woman with isolated facial flushing without diarrhoea or hypertension rarely needs extensive testing, while a patient with episodic flushing, watery diarrhoea, and wheeze requires urgent assessment for carcinoid syndrome. Biochemical screening is the cornerstone for excluding mediator-secreting tumours: 24-hour urine 5-hydroxyindoleacetic acid (5-HIAA) and plasma chromogranin A are the primary tests for carcinoid syndrome; 24-hour urine metanephrines and normetanephrines or plasma-free metanephrines are the preferred test for phaeochromocytoma; serum tryptase, 24-hour urine histamine and prostaglandin D2 metabolites are used for mastocytosis. Blood tests also include fasting glucose to screen for diabetes (which worsens rosacea), thyroid function, and a sex hormone panel to confirm menopausal status. Imaging — CT or MRI of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis — is arranged once biochemistry identifies a neuroendocrine process. Octreotide scintigraphy (somatostatin receptor imaging) or DOTATATE PET-CT localises carcinoid primaries. Skin biopsy confirms mastocytosis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for carcinoid syndrome; 5-HIAA is the serotonin breakdown product secreted in excess by carcinoid tumours; chromogranin A reflects neuroendocrine cell mass and is elevated in most functioning carcinoids.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You collect all urine over 24 hours in an acid-preserved container; dietary serotonin-rich foods (tomatoes, bananas, nuts, avocado) must be avoided for 48 hours before collection. A blood draw for chromogranin A is done the same day.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The most sensitive tests for phaeochromocytoma; plasma-free metanephrines have greater than 97% sensitivity and are the preferred first-line test in specialist guidelines.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw after 20 minutes of rest (supine preferred); a 24-hour urine collection is an alternative. Medication interactions (tricyclic antidepressants, levodopa) must be reviewed before testing.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Elevated baseline tryptase indicates systemic mast cell disease; urine histamine metabolites (methylhistamine) are elevated during mast cell activation episodes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw for tryptase, ideally within 30–60 minutes of a symptomatic flush episode; 24-hour urine collection for histamine metabolites.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms rosacea (telangiectasia, pilosebaceous changes), mastocytosis (urticaria pigmentosa lesions with increased mast cells on biopsy), or other dermatological causes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small (3–4 mm) skin punch taken under local anaesthesia; the site heals with a small scar within 2–3 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Thyroid hormone excess causes sweating and warmth that may accompany flushing; FSH and LH confirm menopausal status in women with vasomotor symptoms.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results available within 24–48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: CT or MRI of abdomen and chest with DOTATATE PET-CT
diagnosticTests.purpose: Localises carcinoid primary tumours and their hepatic metastases, or identifies adrenal phaeochromocytoma, once biochemical screening is positive.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Cross-sectional imaging with contrast; DOTATATE PET-CT requires intravenous radiotracer injection and a full-body scan taking 60–90 minutes at a nuclear medicine facility.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of flushing is directed entirely at the underlying cause, and management differs greatly depending on the diagnosis. For menopausal hot flushes — the most common indication — hormone replacement therapy (HRT) with oestrogen alone (in women without a uterus) or combined oestrogen-progestogen is the most effective treatment, reducing flush frequency by 70–90% compared to placebo in randomised trials, and is recommended for women without contraindications by NICE, NAMS, and the IMS. For those unable to take HRT, prescription non-hormonal options include venlafaxine (an SNRI), paroxetine, gabapentin, and oxybutynin, each supported by trial data showing approximately 50–60% reduction in flush frequency. For rosacea-associated flushing, low-dose doxycycline (40 mg modified-release), topical brimonidine gel (an alpha-2 agonist that constricts dermal vessels), and azelaic acid address the background inflammation and vascular reactivity; trigger avoidance — particularly alcohol, UV exposure, and temperature extremes — is equally important. Carcinoid syndrome is managed with long-acting somatostatin analogues (octreotide LAR or lanreotide), which suppress tumour mediator secretion and reduce flushing episodes by approximately 70% in treated patients; tumour debulking, hepatic embolisation, and everolimus are further options. Phaeochromocytoma is surgically resected after adequate alpha-adrenergic blockade (phenoxybenzamine or doxazosin) to prevent hypertensive crisis during surgery. Mastocytosis flushing is managed with H1 and H2 antihistamines, avoiding mast cell triggers, carrying an epinephrine autoinjector, and in systemic disease, midostaurin or other targeted agents. Drug-induced flushing is addressed by dose reduction, taking aspirin before niacin doses, or switching to an alternative medication.

medicalTreatments.name: Hormone replacement therapy (HRT)
medicalTreatments.description: Oestrogen-containing HRT is the first-line treatment for menopausal hot flushes; combined oestrogen-progestogen is used in women with an intact uterus to prevent endometrial hyperplasia. Transdermal preparations are preferred because they avoid first-pass liver metabolism and have a lower VTE risk than oral oestrogen.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces hot flush frequency by approximately 70–90% compared with placebo in randomised trials; the most effective pharmacological option for vasomotor flushing.

medicalTreatments.name: SNRI/SSRI (venlafaxine, paroxetine)
medicalTreatments.description: Non-hormonal prescription options for menopausal flushing or for women with contraindications to HRT; venlafaxine 37.5–75 mg daily and paroxetine 10–20 mg daily have the most supporting evidence. Paroxetine is the only FDA-approved non-hormonal treatment for menopausal vasomotor symptoms.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces flush frequency by approximately 50–60% versus placebo in clinical trials; less effective than HRT but a useful alternative.

medicalTreatments.name: Somatostatin analogues (octreotide LAR, lanreotide)
medicalTreatments.description: Long-acting injected somatostatin analogues suppress secretion of serotonin, bradykinin, and other mediators from carcinoid tumours, controlling both flushing and diarrhoea. Given as monthly deep subcutaneous or intramuscular injections.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 70% reduction in flushing episodes in carcinoid syndrome patients; also provide modest tumour growth control in approximately 25–50% of cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Alpha-adrenergic blockade (phenoxybenzamine, doxazosin)
medicalTreatments.description: Essential preoperative preparation for phaeochromocytoma, preventing hypertensive crises during surgery by blocking catecholamine-induced vasoconstriction. Phenoxybenzamine is started 10–14 days before surgery; doxazosin is used as an alternative. Beta-blockade is added only after adequate alpha-blockade to avoid paradoxical hypertension.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Near-complete abolition of hypertensive crises during phaeochromocytoma surgery when adequate preoperative blockade is achieved; operative mortality has fallen below 3% in specialist centres.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical brimonidine gel or oxymetazoline for rosacea flushing
medicalTreatments.description: Alpha-2 adrenergic agonist gels cause direct vasoconstriction of superficial dermal vessels, reducing redness and flushing within 30 minutes of application; effects last 8–12 hours per dose. Used for facial redness management in vascular rosacea.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces investigator-assessed redness by at least 1 grade on a validated scale in approximately 20–25% more patients versus vehicle in pivotal trials at 12 hours.

medicalTreatments.name: H1 and H2 antihistamines for mastocytosis
medicalTreatments.description: Cetirizine or loratadine (H1) combined with famotidine or ranitidine (H2) block the itch, flush, and urticarial responses to histamine released during mast cell episodes. The combination of H1 and H2 blockade is more effective than either class alone for histamine-mediated flushing.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Significant symptom improvement in approximately 60–80% of patients with indolent systemic mastocytosis when triggers are also avoided.

surgicalOptions.name: Adrenalectomy for phaeochromocytoma
surgicalOptions.description: Laparoscopic or open surgical removal of the phaeochromocytoma after 10–14 days of alpha-adrenergic blockade is the definitive treatment; bilateral disease may require subtotal adrenalectomy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Cure of catecholamine excess and resolution of flushing in approximately 90% of benign unilateral phaeochromocytoma; recurrence rate approximately 5–10% over 10 years for sporadic tumours.

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical debulking and hepatic procedures for carcinoid syndrome
surgicalOptions.description: Resection of the primary carcinoid tumour, cytoreductive hepatic surgery, or hepatic artery embolisation reduces tumour burden and mediator secretion; indicated when somatostatin analogues alone do not adequately control symptoms.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Significant symptom reduction in approximately 60–70% of appropriately selected patients; hepatic procedures may achieve symptomatic relief for 6–24 months before re-treatment.

alternativeTreatments.name: Trigger identification and avoidance
alternativeTreatments.description: Keeping a flush diary for two to four weeks to identify personal triggers — alcohol, specific foods, temperature changes, emotional stress, or certain skincare products — is the most universally applicable first step and reduces episode frequency without medication in many rosacea and physiological flushing cases.

alternativeTreatments.name: Cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) for menopausal flushes
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured CBT addressing catastrophising thoughts and hypervigilance around hot flushes reduces the distress and perceived severity of flushes in randomised trials, with benefits maintained at 12 months; it is a NICE-recommended non-pharmacological option for women who decline or cannot take HRT.

alternativeTreatments.name: Paced respiration and relaxation techniques
alternativeTreatments.description: Slow diaphragmatic breathing at 6–8 breaths per minute during a flush has been shown in trials to reduce flush intensity and duration; it works by moderating the sympathetic nervous system response that amplifies the vasomotor episode.

whySeeSpecialist: Most benign physiological flushing requires no specialist input. However, a specialist — endocrinologist, gastroenterologist, or dermatologist — should be consulted when flushing occurs more than 5 times daily, when it is accompanied by systemic symptoms (diarrhoea, wheeze, hypertension, headache, urticaria), when it does not correspond to an obvious trigger, or when initial primary care investigation reveals abnormal biochemistry. Phaeochromocytoma and carcinoid syndrome require specialist-led investigation, imaging, and multidisciplinary management. Severe rosacea unresponsive to first-line treatment warrants dermatology input.
doctorSelectionGuide: For menopausal flushing, a GP or menopause specialist familiar with the current HRT evidence base and able to discuss individualised cardiovascular and breast-cancer risk is most useful. For suspected carcinoid or phaeochromocytoma, seek referral to an endocrinologist or gastroenterologist attached to a neuroendocrine tumour (NET) multidisciplinary team with access to specialised nuclear medicine imaging. Rosacea-predominant flushing is best managed by a dermatologist who offers both pharmacological and laser/vascular light-based treatments.
preventionStrategies: Identify and avoid personal flushing triggers by keeping a diary; common culprits are alcohol, hot drinks, spicy food, rapid temperature changes, and intense sunlight.
Apply broad-spectrum SPF 30+ sunscreen daily if you have rosacea; UV radiation is one of the strongest vascular triggers.
Consider evidence-based non-hormonal options or HRT early in the menopause transition, before severe sleep disruption and quality-of-life impairment develop.
Review your medication list with a clinician; stopping or substituting a drug-induced flushing agent can resolve episodes immediately.
Maintain a healthy weight; adiposity worsens vasomotor symptoms by lowering sex hormone-binding globulin and increasing circulating oestrogen fluctuation around the menopause.
Limit alcohol consumption; alcohol is a near-universal flushing trigger in rosacea and in ALDH2-deficient individuals.
lifestyleModifications: Keep ambient temperature moderate — lower room temperature at night, use layered bedding, and use a fan or cooling spray to abort and shorten episodes.
Wear natural, breathable fabrics that wick moisture and do not trap heat against the skin.
Exercise regularly but at moderate intensity; extreme exercise can trigger flushing in susceptible individuals, though regular physical activity reduces overall vasomotor symptom burden.
Manage stress with regular relaxation practice; sympathetic activation from psychological stress lowers the flushing threshold in almost all forms.
Use gentle, fragrance-free skincare formulated for sensitive or rosacea-prone skin; harsh exfoliants, alcohol-based products, and hot water worsen facial vasoreactivity.
Quit smoking; nicotine alters skin vasomotor regulation and aggravates rosacea.

recommended: Cool or room-temperature drinks, which avoid the heat trigger that hot beverages provide
A diet rich in fruit, vegetables, and whole grains, which supports metabolic health and reduces inflammatory mediators
Regular small meals rather than large ones, particularly if alcohol or high-histamine foods are triggers
Adequate hydration, which supports effective thermoregulation and attenuates the intensity of vasomotor episodes
avoid: Alcohol in all forms — a near-universal trigger for rosacea flushing and a potent trigger in ALDH2-deficient individuals
Spicy foods, capsaicin-containing condiments, and hot beverages, which are among the most cited dietary triggers
High-histamine foods (aged cheese, fermented products, processed meats, red wine) in mast cell-related and rosacea flushing
Sodium nitrite-containing cured meats and MSG-rich foods if dietary triggers have been identified in a personal flush diary

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate aerobic exercise — brisk walking, swimming, cycling — is encouraged and helps reduce vasomotor symptom burden over time by improving hypothalamic thermoregulation and cardiovascular fitness. High-intensity exercise in hot environments can trigger flushing in rosacea and menopausal patients; scheduling exercise in the early morning or a cool indoor setting and wearing moisture-wicking clothing minimises this. After adrenalectomy for phaeochromocytoma, exercise is gradually resumed as cleared by the surgical team, typically within 4–6 weeks.
dailyManagement: Keep a flush diary recording the time, duration, triggers, and associated symptoms of each episode; bring this to specialist appointments.
Identify your top three triggers and systematically eliminate them for two weeks to quantify their contribution.
Take any prescribed medication consistently and at the correct time — somatostatin analogues must be injected on schedule to maintain stable blood levels.
Wear a medical alert bracelet if you have a confirmed diagnosis of phaeochromocytoma, carcinoid syndrome, or systemic mastocytosis, and carry an epinephrine autoinjector if advised.
Report any significant increase in flush frequency, new systemic symptoms, or blood pressure readings above 180/110 to your clinician promptly.
prognosis: The prognosis of flushing depends entirely on the underlying cause. Menopausal vasomotor flushing is self-limiting for most women, resolving or diminishing significantly over 2–5 years, though approximately 10% continue to experience flushes for more than a decade. Rosacea is a chronic relapsing condition, but with appropriate trigger avoidance and pharmacological management the majority of patients maintain good skin control. Carcinoid syndrome with liver metastases carries a median survival of approximately 5–8 years in recent population studies, with somatostatin analogues significantly improving symptom control and modest tumour stabilisation. Benign phaeochromocytoma has an excellent prognosis after successful surgical resection, with long-term survival comparable to the general population, though lifelong biochemical monitoring is required for recurrence. Mastocytosis limited to the skin in children often spontaneously resolves by adolescence; adult systemic mastocytosis is managed long-term but indolent forms rarely progress to mast cell leukaemia.
recoveryTimeline: Menopausal hot flushes typically diminish in frequency and severity over 2–5 years from the start of the menopause transition, though HRT provides relief within days to weeks of initiation. Drug-induced flushing resolves within 24–48 hours of stopping or substituting the offending medication. After successful surgical removal of a phaeochromocytoma, catecholamine levels normalise within days and flushing resolves; full recovery from surgery takes 4–6 weeks. Carcinoid syndrome flushing is managed long-term rather than cured; somatostatin analogues reduce episodes within the first weeks of treatment.
complications: Sleep disruption and fatigue from nocturnal sweating and hot flushes, impacting cognitive function, mood, and quality of life in menopausal women.
Permanent facial telangiectasia and skin thickening (rhinophyma) in untreated rosacea from repeated flushing episodes causing chronic vessel dilatation and fibrosis.

Carcinoid heart disease — right-sided valvular damage from prolonged serotonin exposure in carcinoid syndrome, causing tricuspid regurgitation and pulmonary stenosis in 20–50% of patients with advanced disease.
Hypertensive crisis and cardiovascular events (stroke, myocardial infarction) from untreated or undiagnosed phaeochromocytoma, particularly during surgery, childbirth, or physical stress.
Severe hypotensive anaphylactoid reactions in systemic mastocytosis triggered by bee or wasp stings, certain medications, or alcohol without appropriate prophylaxis.
Psychological impact — anxiety, social withdrawal, and reduced quality of life — from frequent, unpredictable, and embarrassing flush episodes regardless of their physical cause.

supportResources.name: British Menopause Society — Hot Flushes and Night Sweats
supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient information on menopausal vasomotor symptoms and treatment options from a UK specialist society.

supportResources.name: National Rosacea Society — Understanding Rosacea
supportResources.description: Patient resources on rosacea triggers, treatment, and the flushing subtype from the leading U.S. rosacea patient advocacy organisation.

supportResources.name: ENETS — European Neuroendocrine Tumour Society Patient Information
supportResources.description: Resources for patients living with carcinoid tumours and carcinoid syndrome, including flushing management and clinical trials.

sources.title: NICE NG23 — Menopause: Diagnosis and Management (2019 update)
Klotz T et al. Pharmacology of Niacin Flushing. European Journal of Clinical Pharmacology, 2011
Kvols LK et al. Treatment of Malignant Carcinoid Syndrome: The Role of Octreotide. New England Journal of Medicine, 1986
Lenders JWM et al. Phaeochromocytoma and Paraganglioma: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline. Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism, 2014
Valent P et al. Standards and Standardisation in Mastocytosis: Consensus Statements on Diagnostics, Treatment Recommendations and Response Criteria. European Journal of Clinical Investigation, 2007
Wilkin JK. Flushing Reactions: Consequences and Mechanisms. Annals of Internal Medicine, 1981
NHS — Rosacea: Overview, Symptoms and Treatment

confusedWithConditions.name: Rosacea
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Rosacea is a specific dermatological diagnosis with characteristic central facial erythema, telangiectasia, and papules; it is one of the most common causes of pathological flushing rather than a mimic of it, but systemic flushing disorders (carcinoid, phaeochromocytoma) must be excluded before attributing chronic flushing to rosacea alone.

confusedWithConditions.name: Anaphylaxis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Anaphylaxis produces flushing alongside urticaria, throat swelling, bronchospasm, and cardiovascular collapse; distinguishing it from other flushing causes is urgent because epinephrine is required immediately — other flushing disorders do not respond to epinephrine.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hyperthyroidism
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Thyrotoxicosis causes sweating, warmth, and flushing alongside weight loss, palpitations, tremor, and a goitre; thyroid function tests distinguish it from other flushing causes, and the presence of these systemic features prompts thyroid investigation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: The butterfly malar rash of SLE is a fixed, photosensitive erythema across the cheeks and nose that persists for days, unlike the episodic transient redness of flushing; ANA and anti-dsDNA antibodies confirm the diagnosis.

relatedConditions.name: Rosacea
relatedConditions.relevance: Rosacea is the most common dermatological condition causing pathological flushing; its management addresses the same neurosvascular hyperreactivity that underlies flush episodes.

relatedConditions.name: Mast cell activation syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Mast cell activation releases histamine and other mediators causing episodic flushing, urticaria, and systemic hypotension; it overlaps with and is distinguished from systemic mastocytosis by tryptase levels and bone marrow findings.

relatedConditions.name: Hyperthyroidism
relatedConditions.relevance: Thyroid hormone excess causes sweating, warmth, and flushing that must be excluded by thyroid function testing when evaluating new-onset flushing symptoms.

relatedConditions.name: Panic disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Panic attacks produce sympathetically mediated flushing, sweating, and palpitations that closely mimic phaeochromocytoma and carcinoid syndrome; normal biochemistry during a symptomatic episode and a psychiatric history help distinguish them.

faqs.question: What causes flushing of the face?
faqs.answer: Facial flushing is caused by sudden widening of skin blood vessels. Common causes include menopause, rosacea, alcohol, spicy foods, anxiety, and exercise. Less common but important causes include carcinoid tumour, phaeochromocytoma, and certain medications. The pattern and triggers of your episodes guide the diagnosis.

faqs.question: When is flushing a sign of something serious?
faqs.answer: Flushing is worth investigating when it occurs without obvious triggers, happens more than 5 times daily, or is accompanied by sweating, diarrhoea, wheeze, headache, hypertension, or palpitations. These features may indicate a hormone-secreting tumour such as carcinoid or phaeochromocytoma and should prompt a blood and urine screen.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a hot flush and flushing?
faqs.answer: A hot flush is a specific type of flushing caused by menopausal oestrogen decline, producing waves of intense heat, sweating, and redness that last 1–5 minutes. Flushing is a broader term for any episodic skin reddening from vasodilation and can have many different causes beyond menopause.

faqs.question: Can alcohol cause flushing?
faqs.answer: Yes. Alcohol causes flushing through direct vasodilation and, in people of East Asian descent with an ALDH2 gene variant, through acetaldehyde accumulation. Alcohol is also one of the strongest triggers for rosacea flushing. Reducing or stopping alcohol often significantly reduces flush frequency.

faqs.question: What tests are done for unexplained flushing?
faqs.answer: For unexplained flushing, doctors typically order 24-hour urine 5-HIAA and plasma chromogranin A to screen for carcinoid syndrome, plasma or urine metanephrines for phaeochromocytoma, serum tryptase for mast cell disease, and thyroid function tests. FSH and LH confirm menopausal status in women.

faqs.question: Is flushing a symptom of carcinoid syndrome?
faqs.answer: Yes. Flushing is the most common symptom of carcinoid syndrome, occurring in about 85% of patients. It is caused by serotonin, bradykinin, and other mediators released by a neuroendocrine tumour. Carcinoid flushing is typically accompanied by diarrhoea and sometimes wheeze — together these three symptoms form the carcinoid triad.

faqs.question: How is menopausal flushing treated?
faqs.answer: Hormone replacement therapy reduces hot flush frequency by 70–90% and is first-line for women without contraindications. Non-hormonal options include the SNRI venlafaxine, paroxetine, and gabapentin, each reducing flushes by around 50–60%. Trigger avoidance, cooler sleeping environments, and CBT provide additional benefit.

faqs.question: Can anxiety cause flushing?
faqs.answer: Yes. Sympathetic nervous system activation during anxiety, panic, or embarrassment causes cutaneous vasodilation and skin redness. Anxiety-related flushing is typically 'wet' — accompanied by sweating — and coincides with a psychologically stressful situation. Normal biochemical tests help distinguish it from tumour-related flushing.

faqs.question: Does rosacea cause flushing?
faqs.answer: Yes. Vascular rosacea (rosacea subtype 1) causes intense episodic facial flushing on a background of persistent central facial redness, triggered by alcohol, heat, UV light, spicy food, and emotional stress. Repeated flushing gradually creates permanent telangiectasia. Treatment with topical brimonidine, low-dose doxycycline, and trigger avoidance controls the episodes.

faqs.question: Can medication cause flushing?
faqs.answer: Yes. Niacin causes prostaglandin-mediated flushing in 70–90% of users; calcium-channel blockers, sildenafil, opioids, and vancomycin are other common causes. The combination of alcohol with metronidazole or disulfiram produces a severe acetaldehyde-related flush. Reviewing your medications with a doctor can identify and resolve drug-induced flushing.

faqs.question: What is the Asian flush reaction?
faqs.answer: The Asian flush is a flushing reaction to alcohol affecting 35–40% of people of East Asian descent. It is caused by an ALDH2 gene variant that impairs breakdown of acetaldehyde, causing it to build up and dilate facial blood vessels. The reaction also includes nausea, rapid heartbeat, and headache, and chronic exposure to acetaldehyde raises oesophageal cancer risk.

faqs.question: Can phaeochromocytoma cause flushing?
faqs.answer: Yes. A phaeochromocytoma — a catecholamine-secreting adrenal tumour — causes episodic flushing alongside severe headache, profuse sweating, and dangerously high blood pressure. Episodes last minutes and are triggered by posture change, straining, or surgery. Plasma-free metanephrines are the most sensitive screening test.

faqs.question: How do I stop flushing?
faqs.answer: The approach depends on the cause. Trigger avoidance reduces rosacea and physiological flushing. HRT or non-hormonal medications control menopausal flushes. Topical brimonidine reduces rosacea redness immediately. Drug-induced flushing stops when the medication is changed. Tumour-related flushing requires treating the underlying condition with somatostatin analogues or surgery.

faqs.question: Is flushing dangerous?
faqs.answer: Benign physiological flushing is harmless. Flushing from phaeochromocytoma can precede a hypertensive crisis with risk of stroke or heart attack. Carcinoid syndrome flushing over years causes valve disease. Mastocytosis flushing can precede life-threatening anaphylactoid collapse. These serious causes are why unexplained frequent flushing with systemic symptoms warrants investigation.

faqs.question: What is a carcinoid flush?
faqs.answer: A carcinoid flush is an episodic reddening of the face, neck, and upper chest caused by serotonin and other mediators released by a carcinoid neuroendocrine tumour. It lasts from seconds to minutes and is often accompanied by diarrhoea and wheeze. Over years, repeated flushing may leave permanent facial discolouration and telangiectasia.

faqs.question: Can diet reduce flushing?
faqs.answer: Yes, for dietary-triggered flushing. Avoiding alcohol, hot drinks, spicy foods, and high-histamine foods reduces episodes in rosacea and mast cell disorders. Keeping a two-week food and symptom diary identifies personal dietary triggers more accurately than general avoidance lists. These dietary changes complement but do not replace medical treatment for systemic causes.

faqs.question: Does flushing affect sleep?
faqs.answer: Menopausal nocturnal sweating and hot flushes are among the most disruptive causes of sleep fragmentation in midlife women, reducing total sleep time and deep sleep. Effective treatment — HRT, SNRIs, or cooling strategies — significantly improves sleep quality, which in turn benefits mood, concentration, and cardiovascular health.

faqs.question: What specialist treats flushing?
faqs.answer: The appropriate specialist depends on the cause: a menopause specialist or GP for vasomotor hot flushes; a dermatologist for rosacea; an endocrinologist for suspected phaeochromocytoma or mastocytosis; a gastroenterologist or NET specialist for suspected carcinoid syndrome. Initial evaluation can always be started by a GP.

faqs.question: Can mastocytosis cause flushing?
faqs.answer: Yes. Mast cell activation in systemic mastocytosis releases histamine and prostaglandins, causing episodic flushing, itching, abdominal cramps, and sometimes hypotension. Triggers include alcohol, insect stings, certain medications, and physical pressure on skin lesions (Darier's sign). H1 and H2 antihistamines and an epinephrine autoinjector are the cornerstone of management.

faqs.question: How long does a flush last?
faqs.answer: Duration varies by cause. A menopausal hot flush typically lasts 1–5 minutes. A rosacea flush lasts seconds to a few minutes but the residual redness persists longer. A carcinoid flush lasts from seconds to 30 minutes or more in severe episodes. A phaeochromocytoma episode lasts 5–60 minutes. Niacin flushing peaks at 15–30 minutes and fades over 1–2 hours.

metaDescription: Flushing causes sudden facial redness and warmth from rosacea, menopause, or systemic disorders. Learn causes, tests, and treatments. Doctor-reviewed.
